---
title: "Coaching spiral 1: Where to start"
author: "Peter Raidl"
draft: True
#image: "Training.png"
---

Recently, I wrote short [article](AI_as_a_coach.qmd) on how the current LLMs can easily write an exercise program. In this post, I made the claim that writing a program is just very easy and given the amount of information out there, it is unsurprising that AI is doing a good job at it.

Writing a good exercise program is just a small part of the job of an exercise scientist or coach, supporing someone during a [training process](WahtsTraining.qmd). Therefore, as a follow-up, I want to go into more detail on what the other tasks are and where to start from.

# The coaching spiral

From a coaching perspective, the training process can be divided into five steps. These steps are forming a (hopefully upward) spiral. The coach has to jump between different steps to keep the spiral moving and keep the athlete engaged. There is no clear starting place in the spiral and depending on your philosophy you might have more emphasise on one step over the other. but in the end you have to run through all of them. Otherwise you are not coaching but merely giving workouts. Nevertheless, there is a center to the spiral and that comes in the form of **Information Exchange**. In coaching information can never flow one way alone. You give and receive information. On the one hand, you provide the right amount and the right kind of information at exactly the right moment. This sounds easier but is a skill in it self. I personally think that most coaches tend to overcoach. Especially in direct face-to-face situations. I am talking about the Jiu-Jitsu coach that explains every detail of a technique for 10min before a 5min drill or the nutrionist explaining the electron-transport chain and the strength coach giving about twenty coaching cues mid-set. These are all examples of overcoaching and probably make everyones life just more difficult than it could be.

You have to gather all information you can get, organize it, manage it and keep it safe. It is a tricky business. Taking the formal and informal information together you can quickly go really deep and completely in the wrong direction. But for now keep it simple: Have an open ear and an open heart combined with a massive assessment toolbox does the trick.

Coaching is not just a straight ladder that leads to a precontemplated and fixed goal. Dan Jon has the saying "The goal is to keep the goal the goal!" - which is one of my favorite quotes ever. In essents he is right. But I think what we should not forget is that in real life goals are not static nor is the person that is trying to get there. It sounds nearly esoteric but coaching someone through a training cycle means that we have to appreciate that everything is connected and fluid. Even we as the coach are not outside of that system but part of it. Coaching means helping someone to grow and grow with them. We have to consistently question our decisions without ever regretting what we did before. All that should happen while having a functioning relationship with our athletes. At some points along the way moral decisions have to be taken. Whatever you think is hard about monitoring or prioritization, take that times ten to understand what some of those decisions feel like. I once had to tell a client that he has a serous issue with alcohol. Another time I had to somehow explain that I will not support a certain goal because of the health risks.

Fundamentally, the exercise program is about execution. At the end of the day the best program is the one that is done consistently[^1]. No matter how hard we study and how many variables we take into account, the most important step and the hardest one is the first. And following ancient Buddhist wisdom, the one step you are currently making is the only one that is certainly important. All others are only memory or hypothetical futures. I truly believe that no matter the context, the goal or the background of the person - just start take the next step. Especially when ambitious , young coaches take over a sport team or a regular client they want to immediately change everything and make everything perfect. They have probably made their own experiances and read a couple of books and papers and want to put it into practice. From an outside perspective, they even might be right. Their approach is more up to the current understanding of training science. Nevertheless, they forget that they are coaches - Meaning their job is to develop with the person and the environment and bring everything together. As a coach you are not sitting outside the training process but you are a part of it.

[^1]: I did not come up with that saying but I do not know the original reference, sorry!
